title: Romulus
subtitle: Legendary founder of Rome
summary: Romulus is the legendary founder and first king of Rome in Roman tradition.
importance: The story of Romulus shaped Roman identity, kingship myths and later ideas about Rome's destiny.
facts: ["Roman tradition dated the foundation of Rome to 753 BCE.", "Romulus and Remus were central figures in Rome's foundation myth.", "The story belongs to mythic tradition, not secure documentary history."]

title: Founding of Rome
subtitle: Traditional beginning of the city
summary: The founding of Rome is traditionally placed in 753 BCE and tied to the Romulus and Remus legend.
importance: It gives Rome a symbolic origin story and anchors later Roman memory.
facts: ["753 BCE is the traditional date, not a secure archaeological event date.", "The foundation myth connected Rome with divine ancestry and heroic violence.", "Later Romans treated the foundation story as part of civic identity."]

title: Roman Kingdom
subtitle: Rome before the Republic
summary: The Roman Kingdom is the traditional monarchic phase of early Rome before the Republic.
importance: It explains the later Roman hatred of kingship and the political memory behind the Republic.
facts: ["The period is partly legendary and difficult to verify in detail.", "Roman tradition lists seven kings before the Republic.", "The expulsion of the last king became a core Republican memory."]

title: Expulsion of the Kings
subtitle: Traditional birth of the Roman Republic
summary: Roman tradition says the monarchy ended after the expulsion of Tarquinius Superbus.
importance: This event became the foundation story of Roman Republican politics.
facts: ["509 BCE is the traditional date for the beginning of the Republic.", "Roman memory linked kingship with tyranny.", "The Republic replaced monarchy with annually elected magistrates."]

title: Roman Republic
subtitle: Rome's republican political system
summary: The Roman Republic was a political system built around magistrates, assemblies, elite competition and the Senate.
importance: It created the military and political machine that conquered the Mediterranean, then collapsed into civil war.
facts: ["The Republic was not a modern democracy.", "The Senate held enormous influence, especially among the elite.", "Military expansion and social conflict helped destabilize the system."]

title: Roman Senate
subtitle: Elite council at the heart of Roman politics
summary: The Roman Senate was a central political body dominated by Rome's elite families.
importance: Understanding the Senate is necessary for understanding the Republic, Caesar and the birth of imperial rule.
facts: ["The Senate was highly influential but changed across Roman history.", "Senators came from Rome's political elite.", "Conflict between commanders and senatorial authority was central to the late Republic."]

title: Hannibal Barca
subtitle: Carthaginian commander and Rome's great enemy
summary: Hannibal Barca was the Carthaginian general who brought the Second Punic War into Italy.
importance: His campaign forced Rome into one of its greatest military crises.
facts: ["Hannibal crossed the Alps with an army during the Second Punic War.", "He won a major victory at Cannae in 216 BCE.", "He was defeated by Scipio Africanus at Zama in 202 BCE."]

title: Second Punic War
subtitle: Rome and Carthage fight for Mediterranean power
summary: The Second Punic War was the great conflict between Rome and Carthage led by figures like Hannibal and Scipio.
importance: Rome's victory made it the dominant power in the western Mediterranean.
facts: ["The war began in 218 BCE and ended in 201 BCE.", "Hannibal fought in Italy for years after crossing the Alps.", "Rome's victory weakened Carthage and expanded Roman dominance."]

title: Battle of Cannae
subtitle: Hannibal's crushing victory over Rome
summary: At Cannae, Hannibal defeated a large Roman army in one of history's most studied battlefield victories.
importance: Cannae became a classic example of encirclement and a symbol of Rome's crisis during the war.
facts: ["The battle took place in 216 BCE during the Second Punic War.", "Hannibal's army defeated a much larger Roman force according to ancient accounts.", "Despite the disaster, Rome refused to surrender."]

title: Scipio Africanus
subtitle: Roman commander who defeated Hannibal
summary: Scipio Africanus was the Roman commander who defeated Hannibal at Zama.
importance: His victory secured Rome's triumph in the Second Punic War.
facts: ["Scipio carried the war into Africa.", "He defeated Hannibal at the Battle of Zama in 202 BCE.", "His victory earned him the agnomen Africanus."]

title: Battle of Zama
subtitle: Rome defeats Hannibal
summary: The Battle of Zama ended with Scipio Africanus defeating Hannibal and forcing Carthage to accept defeat.
importance: Zama ended the Second Punic War and confirmed Rome's rise over Carthage.
facts: ["The battle took place in 202 BCE.", "Scipio Africanus commanded the Roman side.", "Carthage lost the war after Zama."]

title: Julius Caesar
subtitle: General, dictator and destroyer of the Republican balance
summary: Julius Caesar was a Roman general and politician whose rise helped end the Roman Republic.
importance: His civil war, dictatorship and assassination opened the path to imperial rule.
facts: ["Caesar conquered Gaul and gained enormous military prestige.", "He crossed the Rubicon in 49 BCE, beginning civil war against Pompey and the Senate's forces.", "He was assassinated in 44 BCE."]

title: Crossing of the Rubicon
subtitle: Point of no return in Caesar's civil war
summary: Caesar's crossing of the Rubicon marked open defiance of senatorial authority and began civil war.
importance: It became a lasting symbol of irreversible political action.
facts: ["The event is traditionally dated to 49 BCE.", "Crossing with an army broke political norms and challenged Rome's government.", "The act led into Caesar's civil war."]

title: Augustus
subtitle: First Roman emperor
summary: Augustus was Rome's first emperor and the creator of the Principate.
importance: He transformed the broken Republic into a durable imperial system.
facts: ["Augustus was born Gaius Octavius and was Caesar's adopted heir.", "He defeated Antony and Cleopatra's forces after the Battle of Actium.", "His reign began the Roman imperial system."]
